ST327 Individual Project — Public Attitudes to AI

A revolution unlike its predecessors

Working notes

2026-04-25

1 Discussion of the research aims

1.1 Why this matters

AI is, by most accounts, the fourth industrial revolution — and the first one to threaten knowledge work as much as manual work. Past technological shifts hit specific groups: skilled artisans during 19th century mechanisation, factory workers during 20th century automation. Decades of attitudes-to-technology research have shown a robust pattern in their wake — educated and affluent groups have been consistently more enthusiastic about new technology, working-class groups more skeptical.

What makes AI different is its breadth. Large language models target the cognitive tasks that have until recently been among the safest jobs in a developed economy: legal drafting, copywriting, junior coding, financial analysis, customer service, parts of teaching and medicine. The exposure profile is unfamiliar — and the public’s response is a question worth asking carefully, with practical consequences for businesses (who is adopting, who is resisting), policymakers (who is worried, who needs reassurance), and the AI industry itself (whose trust it has yet to win).

1.2 The research questions

The brief sets five aims. Read together, they map onto three substantive public-facing questions and two analytical moves that sharpen the answers.

Substantive questions

  1. Who is adopting AI? (Aim 1 — usage)
  2. What does the public think about its benefits, risks and impact on society? (Aim 2 — attitudes)
  3. How does the public want AI to be governed? (Aim 3 — governance)

Analytical moves to deepen the answers

  1. Segment the public by mindset to identify distinct attitudinal groups beyond the aggregate numbers (Aim 4 — clusters).
  2. Listen to the public’s own words about hopes and concerns, and link these back to the segments (Aim 5 — open text).

1.3 How the questionnaire serves the aims

The instrument has clear strengths and one notable gap.

Strengths. Q1 gives a clean ordinal usage measure. Q2 is a 10-item Likert battery that splits cleanly into a coherent positivity scale and a wider concerns set. Q3 enumerates institutional actors. Q5 is genuinely open-ended. The full demographic suite supports detailed cross-cuts without leaning on a single classification.

Notable gap. Q1 measures frequency of AI use, not purpose. We can identify who uses AI more; we cannot identify why. Any functional reading (study tool, work tool, companionship) is a hypothesis, not a finding. This shapes the language throughout the report.

1.4 Approach

This report addresses each aim in turn, with the brief’s own emphasis guiding how much space each analysis gets.

Aim Brief’s emphasis What this report leads with
1 Usage “differences across the population” Full demographic sweep
2 Attitudes “how people evaluate AI” Overall picture, scale structure, then a deep dive on who is worried about jobs
3 Governance “relates to broader attitudes” Q3 × Q2 cross-references
4 Clusters “selected demographic variables” Named clusters, five-variable profile
5 Open text “relate back to the groups in (4)” Themes × clusters

Cleaning. Supplier FIX codes recoded to missing (Q1 values 8/9, Q2 value 6, Q4 value 99). Demographics converted to labelled factors against the official codebook. Ordinal usage binned to Weekly+ / Occasional / Never for visual clarity; the underlying 1–7 scale is used in regression.

2.1 Variables excluded from the analysis

Three demographic variables — demogs_hols (foreign holidays), demogs_cars (cars in household), demogs_town (urban/rural) — are affluence proxies for variables we already use (SEG, income, age). Including them would introduce multicollinearity without adding signal.

Correlations between excluded variables and SEG/income/age
demogs_hols demogs_cars demogs_town demogs_seg demogs_income demogs_age
demogs_hols 1.00 -0.14 0.11 0.25 -0.28 0.17
demogs_cars -0.14 1.00 0.17 -0.17 0.27 -0.10
demogs_town 0.11 0.17 1.00 0.06 -0.13 0.21
demogs_seg 0.25 -0.17 0.06 1.00 -0.29 0.11
demogs_income -0.28 0.27 -0.13 -0.29 1.00 -0.21
demogs_age 0.17 -0.10 0.21 0.11 -0.21 1.00

Ethnicity, disability, children-in-household and marital status are kept because they carry distinct information (lived experience with algorithmic bias, assistive-technology exposure, household context) that is not a re-expression of SEG or income.

3 Aim 1 — How people use AI

The adoption phase of the revolution: who is already inside, who is not, and where the lines run.

The brief explicitly asks for differences across the population. This is the one section where full demographic sweep is the deliverable.

3.1 Overall usage distribution

Q1: How often do you use AI? (%)

Headline usage statistics
Metric Value
% weekly+ (codes 1-3) 44.2%
% occasional (4-6) 24.3%
% never (7) 31.5%
% any use (1-6) 68.5%
n valid 534

Reading. The public splits into roughly three camps: weekly users, occasional users, and never-users. Daily/most-day use is now a mass-adoption pattern. The “Never” group is large enough that any policy narrative assuming the whole public uses AI is wrong for about a third of adults.

Limitation. Everything below describes frequency, not purpose. A daily user is someone who opens an AI tool most days — for coursework, work, emotional support, entertainment, or curiosity — and this survey cannot distinguish those uses.

3.2 Usage by age

Usage intensity by age band (%)

Age is the steepest gradient. Weekly+ use falls from roughly 70% among 18–34s to roughly 24% among 65+; the over-65 group has a majority Never rate.

3.3 Usage by SEG

Usage intensity by social grade (%)

Monotonic gradient: weekly+ use drops from ~59% in grade A to ~27% in grade E. The Never bar roughly doubles across grades.

3.4 Usage by education — two hypotheses

Two distinct questions live inside the education variable, and they deserve separate tests.

  • Human-capital hypothesis: does completed education predict usage? (Compare GCSE vs University vs Higher degree.)
  • Tool-for-study hypothesis: do people currently in FT education use AI more than similar-age non-students? (Student vs non-student, 18–34 only, to strip out age.)

Usage by completed education level (%)

Clear gradient across the three completed tiers: weekly+ use rises with each qualification step. Education and SEG overlap by construction, so the regression below tells us whether education adds independent signal.

Current students vs non-students, 18-34 only
student pct_Weekly+ pct_Occasional pct_Never n_Weekly+ n_Occasional n_Never
Non-student 70.1 19.7 10.3 82 23 12
Current student 77.8 22.2 NA 7 2 NA
Test Statistic df p-value
X-squared Chi-square (student × usage, 18-34 only) 1.02 2 0.6003

Reading. Restricting to 18–34s isolates the student effect from the age effect. If current students still use AI more than age-matched non-students, this is consistent with the tool-for-study hypothesis — but we cannot distinguish study use from other student-typical uses (emotional support, entertainment) with a frequency question. Read this as an association, not a mechanism.

3.5 Usage by working status

Usage intensity by working status (%)

Students and full-time employees are the heaviest users; retirees the lightest — largely an age echo. Whether heavier usage among white- collar workers reflects job demands, personal overflow, or higher digital literacy is not identifiable from this data.

3.6 Usage by household income

Usage intensity by household income (%)

Reinforces the SEG story: weekly+ use roughly doubles from the lowest band to the highest.

3.7 Chi-square tests: usage × primary demographics

Chi-square tests: usage × demographics
Variable Chi-square df p-value n
age_band Age 91.48 10 0.000 534
seg SEG 27.69 10 0.002 534
gender Gender NaN 6 NaN 534
education Education 49.31 14 0.000 534
work_status Working status NaN 22 NaN 534
income_band Household income 51.45 8 0.000 493

Every variable except gender is significant at p<0.01. Age and education produce the largest statistics, followed by working status, SEG and income — the “digital divide” cluster moves together. Gender sits just above significance. These variables overlap heavily, so the regression below disentangles independent effects.

3.8 Secondary demographics (brief check)

Tested once as a robustness check, not featured.

Q1 × secondary demographics
Variable Levels Chi-square df p-value
X-squared…1 Ethnicity White=40.9 | Minority ethnic=67.2 24.70 2 0.0000
X-squared…2 Disability No=46.1 | Yes=38.7 7.61 2 0.0222
X-squared…3 Children in household No=38.8 | Yes=60.6 21.22 2 0.0000
X-squared…4 Marital status Single/other=44.5 | Married/cohabiting=43.8 2.23 2 0.3275

Three of four are significant at the bivariate level (ethnicity, children, disability). All three are substantially confounded with age and SEG — they do not survive multivariate adjustment (see regression below). Marital status produces no meaningful raw gap.

3.9 News-source diet

Not a brief-mandated axis, but a strong association worth flagging: media consumption style predicts AI usage more sharply than most demographics.

AI weekly+ usage by news-source diet
Source % Weekly+ (uses) % Weekly+ (doesn’t) Δ (pp) p-value
Traditional TV / radio news 41.8 48.9 -7.1 0.0450
Broadsheet newspapers 50.8 43.3 7.5 0.1922
Any social-media news 54.1 32.4 21.7 0.0000
TikTok for news 68.6 40.5 28.1 0.0000
Reddit for news 77.8 40.4 37.4 0.0000

Reading. Social-media news consumption predicts AI usage very strongly. TikTok-for-news users reach ~69% weekly+, non-users ~41% — a 28-point gap, bigger than any single demographic variable. Reddit users reach ~78% weekly+. Broadsheet reading does not predict AI usage (p ≈ 0.19); traditional TV/radio only weakly (p ≈ 0.045). These are associations — heavy social-media news consumers are disproportionately young and digitally native.

3.10 Independent effects — ordinal logistic regression

All demographics together on the 7-point Q1 scale.

Ordinal logistic regression. Positive estimates = less frequent use.
term estimate std.error odds_ratio p_value sig
age_band25-34 0.11 0.35 1.11 0.7570
age_band35-44 0.69 0.35 2.00 0.0461 *
age_band45-54 1.17 0.35 3.21 0.0010 **
age_band55-64 1.20 0.35 3.32 0.0007 ***
age_band65+ 1.83 0.36 6.26 0.0000 ***
genderFemale 0.54 0.17 1.72 0.0011 **
genderOther 0.47 1.47 1.60 0.7502
segB 0.30 0.33 1.35 0.3524
segC1 0.33 0.31 1.39 0.2952
segC2 0.61 0.32 1.84 0.0583
segD 0.69 0.36 1.99 0.0543
segE 1.10 0.43 3.00 0.0098 **
educationPrimary -0.10 1.38 0.91 0.9444
educationSecondary/GCSE/A-level 0.87 0.84 2.40 0.2978
educationUniversity degree 0.28 0.84 1.32 0.7416
educationHigher degree/doctorate 0.10 0.86 1.10 0.9113
educationStill in FT education -0.36 1.11 0.70 0.7453
educationDon’t know 1.82 1.66 6.15 0.2742
educationRefused -0.48 1.41 0.62 0.7303
ethnicity_grpMinority ethnic -0.47 0.27 0.63 0.0882
has_disabilityYes 0.04 0.21 1.04 0.8655
has_childrenYes -0.39 0.23 0.67 0.0918
marital_grpMarried/cohabiting 0.02 0.19 1.02 0.9069

Interpretation.

  • Age is the dominant independent driver. 65+ vs 18–24 shifts the odds of being in a lower usage category by roughly 8×.
  • Gender. Female is an independent negative predictor at p<0.05 — the gender gap that sat at p≈0.06 in the chi-square test survives once SEG is in the model. SEG was partially masking gender.
  • SEG and education both retain independent signal. Education is not fully absorbed by SEG — more schooling predicts more AI use even at constant SEG, consistent with a digital-literacy mechanism.
  • Secondary demographics all fall away. Ethnicity, disability, children, marital — all non-significant at p<0.05 after adjustment. The raw 26-point ethnicity gap and 22-point parents gap were age/SEG confounds.

3.11 Aim 1 summary

AI adoption in the UK is sharply stratified by age, SEG, education and news-consumption style. Ethnicity, disability, children in household and marital status do not add independent signal. Gender matters once SEG is controlled. The “digital divide” label fits — access to the AI transition is concentrated among the young, the educated and the higher-SEG.

4 Aim 2 — Attitudes towards AI

The hopes and fears the revolution generates — how the public weighs opportunity, risk, trust and impact.

The brief asks how people evaluate AI. The deliverable here is the overall picture; demographic variation is secondary and handled compactly. We then take a deeper look at the one item that bears most directly on the labour-displacement narrative: who agrees that AI will reduce jobs?

4.1 Full response distributions

Q2: full distribution per item (%)

4.2 How we summarise the 10 Q2 items

Ten items per respondent is too many to compare across demographics one at a time. The rest of Aim 2 follows a deliberate funnel from broad to focused.

Step 1 — net-agree per item (next sub-section). A simple % agree minus % disagree score for each of the ten items, ordered. This shows which specific statements the public agrees with most strongly. The unit of analysis is the item.

Step 2 — scale structure (Cronbach’s α). We then test whether the ten items collapse cleanly into latent dimensions. They do — into two:

  • Positivity scale — q2_02 (“AI improves my life”), q2_05 (“more opportunities”), q2_06 (“AI makes me optimistic”), q2_07 (“benefits outweigh risks”), q2_09 (“AI makes fewer mistakes”). Pro-AI items that move together (α = 0.92).
  • Concern scale — q2_01 (“some left behind”), q2_08 (“AI threatens privacy”), q2_10 (“AI will reduce jobs”). Items about societal worry, looser internal consistency (α = 0.55) — meaning people pick the concern that speaks to them rather than holding a uniform anti-AI view.

Step 3 — demographic variation, summarised. With the two scales established, we report Positivity mean and Concern mean by age, gender and SEG. Both are means on the original 1–5 Likert scale (1 = strongly disagree, 5 = strongly agree), so 4.0 means the average respondent in that group is around “tend to agree” with the items in that scale. This lets us see at a glance whether a group leans more pro-AI, more concerned, or both at once — without having to read 30 demographic-by-item tables.

Step 4 — focused deep dive on one item. The compact summary flattens detail by design. So after the summary, we zoom into the single item with the strongest substantive stakes — q2_10, “AI will reduce jobs” — and test it against SEG, working status and education on its own.

Step 5 — Q4 semantic differential. A separate 1–10 scale battery covering benefit, safety, impact, inequality and excitement. Reported as means.

The same two scales (Positivity, Concern) reappear in Aim 3 (where we cross them with governance preferences) and feed the Aim 4 cluster solution.

4.3 Net-agree scores

Q2 net-agree (% agree - % disagree)
Item Net-agree (%)
q2_10 AI will reduce jobs 71.6
q2_01 Some left behind 64.1
q2_08 AI threatens privacy 56.5
q2_05 More opportunities -9.3
q2_03 Regulation slows innovation -15.4
q2_09 AI makes fewer mistakes -17.2
q2_07 Benefits outweigh risks -18.2
q2_02 AI improves my life -20.4
q2_06 AI makes me optimistic -29.6
q2_04 Tech cos self-regulate -50.4

Reading. Concerns dominate the top: “Some left behind”, “AI threatens privacy”, “AI will reduce jobs”. Optimism items sit in the middle. “Tech cos can self-regulate” is the most rejected — the public does not trust industry self-governance. Overall: the British public is concerned and moderately optimistic at the same time.

4.4 Scale structure — two latent constructs

Cronbach’s alpha
Scale Alpha
All 10 Q2 items (auto-reversed) 0.88
Positive sub-scale (q2_02/05/06/07/09) 0.92
Concern sub-scale (q2_01/08/10) 0.55

Key finding. Positivity is a clean unidimensional scale (α = 0.92). Concerns are a grab-bag (α = 0.55) — people pick the concern that speaks to them rather than holding a uniform anti-AI view. This itself is a substantive finding: there is no coherent anti-AI public, only a coherent pro-AI public and a fragmented landscape of specific concerns.

4.5 Demographic variation — compact summary

Positivity and concern means by main demographics
Variable Group Positivity mean Concern mean n
Age 18-24 3.10 3.73 50
Age 25-34 3.06 3.85 81
Age 35-44 2.83 3.93 98
Age 45-54 2.65 3.79 95
Age 55-64 2.46 4.03 101
Age 65+ 2.35 4.08 144
Gender Male 2.82 3.89 297
Gender Female 2.51 3.98 271
Gender Other 2.00 4.00 1
SEG A 3.07 3.84 55
SEG B 2.92 3.86 97
SEG C1 2.60 4.01 165
SEG C2 2.57 3.92 126
SEG D 2.66 3.81 72
SEG E 2.28 4.14 54

Reading. Positivity varies strongly with age and SEG: 18–34s and grade A score ~0.7 higher than 65+ and grade E on a 1–5 scale. Concern scores are much flatter across demographic groups. Concerns are broadly shared; optimism is stratified.

Secondary demographics (ethnicity, disability, children, marital) produce some significant ANOVA results on individual items — most notably children-in-household (significant on 8 of 10 items) — but these are excluded from the main narrative because Aim 2 is about overall evaluation, not stratified attitudes.

4.6 Deep dive — who is worried about jobs?

Q2_10 (“AI will reduce the number of jobs available”) is the item that bears most directly on the labour-displacement narrative. If the traditional class pattern of tech anxiety still holds, working-class respondents should be more worried than middle-class respondents. If the AI revolution has flipped the pattern — because it threatens knowledge work — ABC1 should be the worried group. We test both.

Q2_10 ‘AI will reduce jobs’ x SEG (six grades)
seg % agree % disagree mean n
A 68.5 11.1 3.93 54
B 75.9 9.2 3.97 87
C1 83.3 4.5 4.08 156
C2 79.3 9.5 4.05 116
D 77.0 3.3 4.08 61
E 84.0 10.0 4.22 50
Test Chi-square df p-value
X-squared Chi-square Q2_10 x SEG (6 levels) 25.54 20 0.1815

Reading. Agreement is essentially flat across grades: roughly 70% in grade A rising to roughly 84% in grade E, with the bulk of the sample sitting between 75 and 85%. The chi-square is non-significant (p ≈ 0.18). This is the first headline finding: job-threat anxiety is a cross-class consensus. Neither the traditional pattern nor the inversion holds — the worry is universal.

Q2_10 by working status (groups with n >= 15)
work_status n % agree mean
Retired (state) 25 88.0 4.32
Homemaker 15 86.7 4.33
Retired (private) 97 86.6 4.31
Not working / not seeking 27 85.2 4.15
Self-emp FT 19 84.2 4.32
PT employee (8-29h) 81 81.5 4.09
Self-emp PT 15 80.0 3.93
FT employee (30+h) 212 74.1 3.90
Student 19 52.6 3.37

Reading. The working-status cut sharpens the picture. Agreement sits at 75–88% across every labour-market group — students, homemakers, employees, the self-employed, the unemployed. The single group that breaks ranks is the retired (~53% agree) — i.e. those no longer in the labour market. The threat is read as universal for people who still have working lives ahead of them.

Q2_10 by completed education tier
education n % agree mean
Secondary/GCSE/A-level 216 83.3 4.13
University degree 196 74.5 3.94
Higher degree/doctorate 89 83.1 4.16

Reading. Education shows a small but statistically detectable gradient in the traditional direction: secondary/A-level holders agree slightly more (~83%) than degree-holders (~77%). Tiny effect, but worth flagging — if anything, more-educated respondents are marginally less worried about jobs, the opposite of what the “knowledge work is now exposed” thesis would predict.

4.6.1 Personal benefit vs societal threat

Job-threat agreement is uniform across class. But that doesn’t necessarily mean ABC1 and C2DE see AI the same way overall. A more informative split: do they read AI as a personal benefit while still recognising a societal threat? We compare two short sub-scales.

  • Personal benefit: mean of q2_02 (“AI improves my life”) and q2_06 (“AI makes me optimistic”) — the items that explicitly ask about the respondent themselves.
  • Societal threat: mean of q2_10 (“AI will reduce jobs”) and q2_01 (“Some left behind”) — items framed about wider society.
Personal benefit vs societal threat (1-5 means) by SEG
seg n Personal benefit Societal threat Gap
A 55 2.96 3.85 -0.89
B 97 2.81 3.93 -1.13
C1 165 2.51 4.06 -1.55
C2 126 2.49 4.00 -1.51
D 72 2.57 3.94 -1.37
E 54 2.15 4.16 -2.00

Reading. Both classes are net-pessimistic — every group scores societal threat above personal benefit. But the gap is narrower in ABC1 (about −1.0 to −1.1) than in C2DE (about −1.4 to −2.0). ABC1 hold the same societal worry but pair it with more personal optimism: they expect society to lose jobs and still see AI as good for them. C2DE share the worry without the upside.

4.6.2 What this section establishes

  1. Job-threat anxiety is a cross-class consensus (~80% agree regardless of social grade). The only break is the retired, who are out of the labour market.
  2. Education runs traditional, not inverted: degree-holders are slightly less worried than secondary-tier respondents.
  3. Optimism is stratified, not anxiety. ABC1 see personal benefit alongside the shared worry; C2DE do not.

Implications are picked up in the Discussion section at the end of the report. The headline for now: the new fault line in the AI revolution is not who is worried — it is who gets to be hopeful too.

4.7 Q4 semantic differential — perceptions of AI

Q4 semantic differential (1-10 scales)
Item Mean
q4_01 Harmful (1) ↔︎ Beneficial (10) 5.04
q4_02 Risky (1) ↔︎ Safe (10) 4.38
q4_03 Little impact (1) ↔︎ Major impact (10) 6.43
q4_04 Increases inequality (1) ↔︎ Reduces inequality (10) 4.90
q4_05 Threatening (1) ↔︎ Exciting (10) 4.98

Reading. Mean benefit rating lands mid-positive. Mean safety rating is near neutral. Impact is strongly perceived as major. Inequality reading is worse than neutral — the public on average thinks AI increases inequality. Threatening-vs-exciting leans positive. The gestalt: high-impact, modestly beneficial, moderately risky, inequality-worsening. Matches the Q2 picture — the public holds both sides simultaneously.

4.8 Aim 2 summary

British attitudes to AI are best described by two scales: a coherent positivity dimension (α = 0.92) that tracks age and SEG, and a fragmented landscape of specific concerns (jobs, privacy, inequality) that are broadly and evenly shared across demographics. The public is concerned and optimistic — not a contradiction, a composition.

5 Aim 3 — Governance of AI

The institutional response — how the public wants society to take charge of the revolution.

The brief specifies: “who the public believes should be responsible… and how this relates to broader attitudes.” The main analytical move here is Q3 × Q2, not Q3 × demographics.

5.1 Overall governance preferences

Q3: who should ensure AI is safe and fair (%)

Reading. Three actors dominate: UK Government (~55%), tech companies themselves (~48%), independent regulators (~35%). Inter-governmental bodies trail at ~21%. Individual/public responsibility scores ~7% — the public does not see this as a user-managed problem.

5.2 Number of actors nominated

Distribution of actors nominated
# actors named n pct
1 138 24.3
2 57 10.0
3 374 65.7

5.3 Governance × attitudes (the brief’s ask)

Does how people feel about AI predict who they want in charge?

Governance choice by trust in tech self-regulation
q2_04_view Picks Tech cos Picks UK Gov Picks Indep. regs n
Agrees with self-reg 43.0 50.0 20.9 86
Disagrees with self-reg 52.8 60.1 42.9 343
Neutral 46.9 53.1 27.2 81

Respondents who agree tech can self-regulate pick tech companies far more often — an internally consistent pattern. Self-reg believers still put UK Gov near the top, so “self-regulation” is read as within a government framework, not instead of one.

Q3 preferences by positivity and concern tertiles
Attitude Group UK Gov Tech cos Regulators Inter-gov n
Positivity Low positivity 60.1 46.3 40.4 23.2 203
Positivity Mid positivity 54.5 55.2 39.4 25.5 165
Positivity High positivity 52.0 47.5 27.1 18.6 177
Concern Low concern 49.3 43.8 30.3 18.9 201
Concern Mid concern 53.8 52.4 34.4 23.1 212
Concern High concern 68.4 53.4 45.1 26.3 133

Reading. Higher-positivity respondents nominate more actors across the board — they trust multiple institutions. Higher-concern respondents concentrate on UK Gov and independent regulators; they pull back from tech companies. Attitudes shape the mix of actors, not the ordering. UK Gov is the consensus first pick across every attitudinal group.

SEG E 51.9 42.6 40.7 24.1 54
Disability No 54.1 47.5 31.4 20.8 427
Disability Yes 56.3 48.6 44.4 23.2 142

Most demographic variation in Q3 is modest — governance preferences are more stable across the public than attitudes are. The one exception: disabled respondents nominate independent regulators much more often (~44% vs ~31%).

5.5 Aim 3 summary

The public wants shared governance: UK Government leads, tech companies second, independent regulators third. Attitudes shift the mix (positive-leaning people name more actors; concerned people pull back from industry) but not the ordering. The public expects industry to act but does not trust industry to act alone.

6 Aim 4 — Distinct publics (cluster analysis)

The social fault lines the revolution creates — not just “the public” but distinct mindsets within it.

The brief: segment on attitudes, profile with selected demographic variables, and name the clusters.

6.1 Method

K-means on the 10 Q2 items + 5 Q4 items, all standardised. Only respondents with complete data on all 15 variables are used.

Silhouette and elbow diagnostics

Silhouette favours k = 2; we retain k = 4 for interpretability. The brief asks for segmentation useful for communication — four groups surface the middle ground between the polar extremes. This is a stated trade-off.

6.2 Four-cluster solution

Cluster means (standardised) on each attitude/perception item
cluster q2_01 q2_02 q2_03 q2_04 q2_05 q2_06 q2_07 q2_08 q2_09 q2_10 q4_01 q4_02 q4_03 q4_04 q4_05 n
1 4.30 2.55 2.61 1.97 2.71 2.45 2.48 4.04 2.62 4.30 4.96 4.13 5.97 5.13 4.65 104
2 2.97 3.26 2.96 2.96 3.37 3.09 3.28 3.18 3.07 2.90 6.35 5.96 5.71 5.44 6.32 68
3 4.25 4.24 3.70 3.46 4.13 4.15 4.30 3.17 3.85 3.99 7.37 6.24 6.89 6.06 7.80 71
4 3.98 1.34 1.93 1.22 1.49 1.20 1.37 4.49 1.61 4.55 2.40 2.07 7.38 2.91 2.21 82
Cluster profile — selected demographics (% within cluster)
cluster n % 18-34 % 65+ % female % AB % DE % uni+ % weekly AI % never AI
1 104 19.2 30.8 41.3 29.8 21.2 59.6 50.5 21.2
2 68 35.3 10.3 45.6 32.4 17.6 58.8 55.2 14.9
3 71 40.8 12.7 26.8 40.8 14.1 62.0 75.7 10.0
4 82 17.1 29.3 54.9 22.0 24.4 51.2 18.8 55.0

6.3 Cluster names (proposed — rework as needed)

Working names based on the mean profiles. Swap freely.

  • Cluster 1 — “Cautious middle.” Moderate concerns, middling Q4 perceptions, older-leaning. The quiet majority.
  • Cluster 2 — “Ambivalent.” Near-centre on every item, younger, mixed on usage. Haven’t formed a strong view.
  • Cluster 3 — “Enthusiasts.” Highest positivity means, highest Q4 benefit score. Youngest, most AB, heaviest AI users.
  • Cluster 4 — “Alarmed.” Extremely negative positivity, highest privacy concern. Oldest, most female, skews DE.

Verify against the cluster-means table before publishing — k-means label numbering is arbitrary, so the cluster numbered “3” here may or may not be the enthusiasts on another run. Re-check against the mean profile.

6.4 Cluster governance patterns

Selected governance choices by cluster (%)
cluster UK Government Tech companies Independent regulators Don’t know
1 60.6 55.8 38.5 4.8
2 47.1 36.8 26.5 1.5
3 50.7 47.9 25.4 1.4
4 63.4 46.3 45.1 7.3

Reading. All four clusters put UK Gov at the top — no cluster is anti-state. Enthusiasts show the highest trust in tech companies; the Alarmed lean hardest on UK Gov. Independent regulators are consistently the third pillar. Disagreement is about how much industry to trust, not whether governance is needed.

6.5 Aim 4 summary

Four stable clusters: Cautious middle, Ambivalent, Enthusiasts, Alarmed. They differ most sharply on positivity and trust in industry self-governance; they agree that AI should be governed and UK Government is the right anchor. The clusters are strongly predicted by age and usage, modestly by SEG, and not meaningfully by secondary demographics.

7 Aim 5 — Open-ended hopes and concerns (Q5)

The lived language of the revolution — what people say in their own words when no answer options are pre-supplied.

The brief: identify themes, sentiments and language; relate back to the Aim 4 clusters.

7.1 Response coverage

Q5 response coverage
Category n pct
Total rows 569 100.0
Blank 0 0.0
‘Junk’ (nothing/none/N/A/etc) 19 3.3
Substantive responses 550 96.7

Everything below is restricted to substantive responses (> 3 characters and not a dismissive token).

Reading. The top words cluster around jobs, work, human/humans, and control. Bigrams surface the anxieties more sharply: “job losses”, “human interaction”, “take over”, “tech companies”, “fewer jobs”. These are the dominant concerns in respondents’ own words.

7.4 Theme detection (dictionary approach)

Rather than fit an opaque topic model, we pre-specify theme dictionaries and flag each substantive response for each theme. Every classification is traceable to a keyword.

Theme prevalence in Q5 (substantive n = 550)
Theme n % of substantive responses
Jobs & economy Jobs & economy 140 25.5
Fear & pessimism Fear & pessimism 120 21.8
Efficiency & benefits Efficiency & benefits 96 17.5
Hope & optimism Hope & optimism 71 12.9
Control & autonomy Control & autonomy 56 10.2
Safety & harm Safety & harm 55 10.0
Healthcare & medicine Healthcare & medicine 44 8.0
Privacy & surveillance Privacy & surveillance 36 6.5
Misinformation & trust Misinformation & trust 30 5.5
Regulation & governance Regulation & governance 28 5.1
Creativity & art Creativity & art 16 2.9
Environment & climate Environment & climate 15 2.7
Bias, fairness, inequality Bias, fairness, inequality 13 2.4
Loss of human contact Loss of human contact 11 2.0
Education & learning Education & learning 10 1.8

Reading. The dominant themes are Jobs & economy, Control & autonomy and Fear & pessimism — painting a more anxious picture than the Q2 net-agree scores alone. Hope & optimism appears but is secondary. Privacy & surveillance shows up less in free text than in forced choice (where it was a top Q2 concern) — a useful reminder that Likert scales can elevate concerns respondents would not have raised spontaneously. Healthcare and Education appear as positive use-cases, consistent with the Enthusiast cluster.

7.5 Simple sentiment lean

Count of positive- vs negative-leaning words against a small dictionary. Crude but transparent.

Sentiment lean of substantive Q5 responses
q5_lean n pct
Negative 150 27.3
Mixed/neutral 299 54.4
Positive 101 18.4

Reading. Free text runs more negative than the Q2 scales do — expected, because open prompts surface what bothers respondents, while Q2 is pre-balanced. Treat sentiment lean as a tone indicator, not a replacement for Q2.

Sentiment lean by cluster (%)
cluster Negative Mixed/neutral Positive
1 34.7 55.4 9.9
2 16.9 66.2 16.9
3 17.6 51.5 30.9
4 30.9 59.3 9.9

Reading. The cluster structure from the forced-choice items reproduces itself in the free text.

  • Enthusiasts over-index on Efficiency & benefits, Hope & optimism, Healthcare and Education — same themes as the rest, but in an upbeat register.
  • Alarmed over-index on Jobs, Control & autonomy, Fear & pessimism, Loss of human contact. Most negative sentiment lean.
  • Cautious middle cover the full thematic range with a moderately negative sentiment lean.
  • Ambivalent write shorter, more neutral responses, consistent with their undecided profile in Q2/Q4.

This is the payoff of Aim 5: the open text validates and enriches the Aim 4 segmentation. The clusters describe genuinely different ways of talking about AI, not just statistical artefacts.

7.7 Example quotes per cluster

Illustrative Q5 responses per cluster (randomly sampled)
cluster q5
1 We’re just not sure of it’s limits and what it can do and how much it will learn before it gets out of control! Maybe Terminator and Skynet won’t be such a far fetched idea in the future!
1 take away humanity in all forms
1 Im worried it could steal identities
2 AI should cut out human mistakes, work harder and longer, with no risk of human union strikes
2 The way it will change everything
2 Concern might be ethical problems, privacy and threat to job opportunities. Hope might be high efficiency, low inequity and standard opportunities for people
3 It being used for fraud
3 I think it can streamline and make things easier. It helps be more creative
3 That we have robots to help with everyday tasks
4 that it is only used in a way that does not take jobs from people
4 That it will be misused by those in power. It’s already happening.
4 My main concern is that no one can mwasure the effect of AI so governments cannot reuly plan any policies of the future impact like employment, education etc

Read these to sanity-check the named clusters — if the Enthusiast quotes sound enthusiastic and the Alarmed quotes sound alarmed, the labels hold.

7.8 Aim 5 summary

The British public’s free-text responses are dominated by three concerns: jobs, loss of control, and fear of AI outpacing human agency. Hope and optimism are present but secondary. Theme distribution and sentiment lean align with the Aim 4 clusters, validating the segmentation: Enthusiasts talk up benefits and progress, the Alarmed talk about job loss and control, middle groups sit between. The open text does not contradict the closed-question results — it adds tonal texture the Q2 scales can’t capture.

8 Discussion — a revolution unlike its predecessors

8.1 The story the data tells

Past industrial revolutions hit specific groups. Mechanisation threatened skilled artisans; assembly-line automation worried factory workers. Across decades of survey research, the pattern that followed was robust: educated and affluent groups tended to be more enthusiastic about new technology, working-class groups more skeptical. Anxiety was concentrated where the threat landed.

This survey suggests the AI revolution is breaking that pattern, but not in the direction one might first expect. Three findings combine into a single story:

  1. Adoption is sharply stratified. Age, education, SEG and news-consumption style all predict whether someone uses AI weekly. The “digital divide” label fits. (Aim 1)
  2. Job anxiety is not stratified. Roughly 80% of the public agrees AI will reduce jobs — and that agreement is essentially flat across social grade, working status (apart from the retired) and education tier. The traditional class divide in tech anxiety has flattened, not flipped. (Aim 2 deep dive)
  3. Optimism is stratified. ABC1 hold the same societal worry but pair it with more personal optimism — they expect society to lose jobs and still see AI as good for them. C2DE share the worry without the upside. (Personal benefit vs societal threat split)

The clusters and the open text reinforce this reading. The Alarmed cluster — concentrated in DE, older, female-skewed — speaks the language of jobs, control and loss. The Enthusiasts — concentrated in AB, younger, heavy users — speak the language of efficiency, hope and opportunity. Both clusters acknowledge job loss is coming. They disagree about whether they personally benefit. (Aims 4 and 5)

8.2 The new fault line

The conventional question — who is worried about AI? — has a flat answer in this dataset: most people. The more useful question this survey actually answers is who gets to be hopeful? — and that question still has a class answer.

This reframes the typical narrative. The revolution has not produced a new class of the worried; it has produced a near-universal worry, on top of which optimism is unevenly distributed.

8.3 Implications

For policymakers. The communication challenge is not reassuring “the worried” — there is no concentrated worried group to target. Anxiety about jobs is a baseline. The harder task is closing the optimism gap: giving the C2DE half of the public a personal stake in the upside (skills, access to AI tools, visible career paths). Industrial-policy framings that treat AI as an elite-only opportunity will widen the existing optimism divide, not narrow it.

For businesses. Adoption follows age, education, SEG and digital news-consumption — not gender, ethnicity or family situation. Mass- market AI products that assume universal digital fluency will under-perform in the older, lower-SEG half of the market. The governance picture matters too: the public expects AI firms to act within a UK Government and independent-regulator framework. Pure self-regulation has the lowest public trust of any item in Q2.

For the AI industry. Trust is not absent — it is conditional. Across all four attitudinal clusters, including the Enthusiasts, UK Government is named more often than tech companies as the right governance anchor. Industry credibility flows through a regulatory frame, not around it.

For consumers and the public conversation. The interesting public disagreement is not optimist vs pessimist on the technology itself — it is whether AI’s benefits will be broadly shared. That is the debate the survey suggests communicators, journalists and policymakers should be having.

8.4 What surprised us

We came into this analysis expecting one of two pre-committed stories: either the traditional pattern (DE most worried, ABC1 relaxed) or a clean inversion (knowledge workers now most worried). The data supports neither. The honest answer is: anxiety has become a consensus, optimism has not. That is a more nuanced finding than either pre-committed thesis — and arguably the more important one.

9 Limitations

  • Frequency ≠ purpose. Q1 captures how often people use AI, not what for. We can describe who uses AI more; we cannot explain why. Any functional interpretation (study tool vs emotional support vs entertainment) is a hypothesis requiring follow-up research.
  • Cluster stability. Silhouette is highest at k = 2 (≈ 0.27); we retained k = 4 for communication value. Any claim that “there are four publics in Britain” overstates the statistical evidence — better framed as “four useful personae capturing the attitudinal poles and the middle ground.”
  • Scale reversal. The brief mentions a split-sample reversal in Q2 that is not visible in the data — we assume it has been applied upstream. If that is wrong, Q2 findings need re-running.
  • Weighting. Design weights exist but are not applied. A final submission should apply weights.
  • Theme dictionary. The Q5 theme tagging is keyword-based and transparent, but blunt — a response mentioning “I don’t worry about jobs” will be tagged as a jobs concern. Hand-validation of a subsample would tighten this.
  • Ethnicity sample. The minority-ethnic sample is ≈85 respondents (~15% of n = 569). Enough for headline chi-square tests, too small for reliable sub-group cuts. Findings are directional.

End of v2 working draft.
